I’ve been interested for a long time in biomimetic sensor processing. For example, humans don’t really work on frame buffers the way computers do. Input frames get transformed into bundles of lightly processed information…

![][image1]

M-cells \- motion (low spatial, high temporal resolution)  
P-cells \- color/contrast (small size, high spatial resolution)  
K-cells \- low-light sensitivity  
Direction-selective \- not just motion…direction of motion  
Orientation-selective \- edges  
Luminance/Contrast

Many experiments…can we pick out saliency/novelty, risk, prediction…?

Yes, we can.  
![][image2]  
![][image3]![][image4]

You can what’s happening with internals…the frame buffers disappear…they are transformed into bundles, and the bundles move forward as, effectively, embedding vectors. Decisions are made based on \*those\*...not on the actual video. And feedback is a natural byproduct of this…a kind of hypervisor can choose what matters in that moment, and it pushes gain/attenuation back up stream. From there…it’s a small step to unsupervised “wander the world” learning…

That’s super fun, but I was marginal on compute power for video. Then we were watching an old episode of Hell’s Kitchen…where the chefs do the Palette test…where it turns out a trained, professional chef, when blindfolded, often can’t tell the difference between beef and chicken…

It got me thinking about aromas. Does a similar “bundling” thing happening in the olfactory pathways? Yep…the nose has many “sensors” in relatively few classes…and they are all wired together in a kind of feed forward network producing intermediate representations…ie, embeddings. It’s the embeddings that reach our bioLLM, not the sensor data.

The way it works is…

Scent \-\> Nose \-\> Nose sends “floral” to brain …  
Brain says “oh\! Rose?” back to nose…  
Nose adjusts gain/attentuation on sensors to focus on the constituents that should trigger on “rose”

So…is there a taxonomy for smells? Yep. Here is a standard one.

![][image5]

The next question…is there a small, cheap set of sensors I can play with? 

Yep. Gotta love AliExpress….$20. Standard gas sensors tuned for different volatiles.

![][image6]  
Now…can we map sensor response to each fragrance note…? Also Yep.

![][image7]

So now we have something maybe we can play with. If we look at the response curves a little differently…

![][image8]

Now you really start to see it. And what about timing…?

![][image9]

And if we quantize the peaks…?

![][image10]

And there it is, right? Even just visually, from this, you can see that there should be a relatively painless mapping between input scent and output label. Right? Which means we should be able to do something like this…

![][image11]  
What’s happening here…

14 fragrance notes \-\> 8 sensors \-\> 8 ADC streams \-\> 8x8 spatiotemporal grid.

At each time quanta, the streams are blown out into what I call the accordion…8 \-\> 64 mapping...something like this, which evolves as understanding grows…

**p00-p15: Single-Lane Detectors**  
One motif per input stream. These are simple “this channel is active” detectors.

* p00-p07: single rate/amplitude sensor lanes  
* p08-p15: single latency/delta sensor lanes

They preserve raw evidence before any combinatorics.

**p16-p31: Pair Coactivation Motifs**  
These detect selected two-sensor relationships: “these two sensors are active together.” Examples include `adc0+adc2`, `adc1+adc7`, `adc4+adc6`.

Purpose: capture chemically meaningful pair signatures without hard-coding fragrance labels.

**p32-p47: Onset \+ Tail Motifs**  
These combine fast transient evidence from some sensors with sustained tail evidence from others.

Purpose: distinguish “sharp top-note arrival plus sticky base-channel persistence” from simple coactivation.

**p48-p63: Cluster History Motifs**  
These are broader 2-, 3-, and 4-sensor clusters with mixed rate/latency weighting.

Purpose: catch larger family-level patterns: fresh/floral/amber/woody-style sensor constellations, still without directly encoding label rules.

And this all works…

 [http://127.0.0.1:7890/](http://127.0.0.1:7890/)

The model is straight Rust. It is small, it is fast, and it is designed specifically to run well on this hardware…

![][image12]

That is an ESP32 S3. In volume, It’s a $1 computer, 1cm square. 

And I am currently replacing the sensor package with this…

![][image13]   
Honestly, if I pull this off…graphite (ie pencil) on alumina silica with gaps doped with metal oxide liquids and then baked. It “should” work. Then we have a sensor smaller than the ESP32 and the whole package is, like, $2, ultra low power, and emitting standard embeddings. So it drops right into normal LLMs as a normal multimodal element.

Now I want to say a bit about Daft. I had no idea what it was until I spoke with Jay. I thought…ok…I’m already having issues managing the datasets…maybe Daft can help. A few hours later…

Daft is integrated as the dataset materialization layer, not as part of the Rust model runtime. The idea is that Daft owns the “what data are we training/testing on?” question, while Rust owns the “how does the model train/infer?” question.

* Recipes such as `recipes/peak_single_note.toml` define selection criteria: label counts, per-label limits, included/excluded capture classes, and output location.  
* `scripts/materialize_dataset.sh` builds concrete dataset views under `data/views/...`.  
* The Rust trainers consume those materialized CSV views directly, so model code stays independent of Daft.  
* This lets us rebuild 0-note, 1-note, 2-note, 3-note, smoke, demo, or focused datasets consistently from the same raw/synthetic source pool.

Daft also gives us a ledgering path: each materialization and test run can produce manifests/results that describe what was selected, what was run, and what happened. That matters because the synthetic space is now large enough that “which data did we train on?” is part of the experiment.

* `data/manifest/captures.csv` acts as the capture inventory.  
* View manifests such as `data/views/..._manifest.csv` record the selected subset.  
* Test/result CSVs can be joined back against the manifests for post-run analysis.  
* Longer term, this gives us a clean audit trail from raw/generated captures \-\> materialized training view \-\> model output \-\> evaluation results.

I’m maintaining a strict boundary between Model and Everything else. Model has to be performant \- it is Rust. Training wrapper and usability UX \- I don’t care so much.
